original_abstract: Atomistic simulations are indispensable tools for fields ranging from drug discovery to materials science. Recently, machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have emerged as powerful alternatives to costly ab initio calculations. A transformative advancement for the field would be the development of a "universal" reactive MLIP—one capable of accurately modeling diverse chemical transformations across various environments without requiring system-specific retraining. In this work, we introduce ANI-1xnr, a general-purpose reactive MLIP developed through the automated sampling of chemical reactions in condensed phases. To demonstrate its versatility, we applied ANI-1xnr to five complex and distinct chemical processes: the nucleation of solid-phase carbon, the formation of graphene rings from acetylene, the degradation of biofuel additives, methane combustion, and the prebiotic synthesis of glycine from small molecules. In each case, ANI-1xnr produced results in close agreement with experimental data and established theoretical benchmarks. Our findings suggest that ANI-1xnr is a robust and highly transferable model for C, H, N, and O chemistry, providing a scalable foundation for high-throughput in silico exploration of reactive systems.
humanized_abstract: Atomistic simulations are widely used in drug design, catalysis, soft matter physics and solid state physics, to name a few. With the advancement of machine learning, interatomic potentials (IAPs) based on machine learning (ML) approach, namely, machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs), have attracted increasing attention due to the fact that they are much faster than ab initio methods and still retain good accuracy. An universal (or general purpose) reactive MLIP that does not need to be re-trained for different systems is always highly desired. In this work, we report a general-purpose reactive MLIP, ANI-1xnr, which is constructed by learning potentials from the data generated by the automated sampling of chemical reactions in condensed phases. We then apply ANI-1xnr to model five distinct reactions, involving solid-phase carbon nucleation, the formation of graphene rings from acetylene, biofuel additives degradation, methane combustion, and prebiotic formation of glycine from small molecules. The results are compared with experimental data and other theoretical results, demonstrating the accuracy and applicability of ANI-1xnr. We conclude that ANI-1xnr can be used as a general-purpose model to study C, H, N and O elements at the atomistic scale, which can pave the way for high-throughput simulations of reactive systems in different fields.
